title: Listening - Part 4: Downsizing and Simpler Living
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five individuals who chose to significantly downsize their lifestyle and possessions

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: We were living in a five-bedroom house, and [21]the sheer amount of time and money spent on maintenance was starting to feel like a burden[/21]. We weren't using half the rooms! Moving into a tiny apartment was a shock, but [26]the financial freedom we've gained by eliminating a massive mortgage has been life-changing[/26]. We can finally afford to travel and pursue our hobbies without checking our bank balance every day. It’s like a weight has been lifted off our shoulders, allowing us to focus on experiences rather than possessions. || Speaker 2: I used to be a shopaholic, always chasing the latest trends. [22]I decided to downsize because I felt suffocated by the clutter in my flat and the constant need to buy more[/22]. Clearing everything out was surprisingly emotional, but [27]the mental clarity that comes from living in a clean, minimalist space is incredible[/27]. I no longer spend my weekends tidying up or shopping for things I don't need. It’s given me so much more mental energy to focus on my creative work and my relationships, which are far more fulfilling than any designer handbag. || Speaker 3: After my kids left for university, the house felt empty and silent. [23]I chose to move onto a canal boat because I wanted a lifestyle that was more in tune with the natural world[/23]. It’s a small space, but the view changes every day. [28]What I appreciate most is the strong sense of camaraderie among the boating community[/28]. Everyone helps each other out with repairs or advice. It’s a level of social connection I never had in the suburbs, where people barely waved over their fences. It’s been a wonderful new chapter. || Speaker 4: I was working eighty hours a week to pay for a lifestyle I didn't even have time to enjoy. [24]I downsized to a smaller home and a part-time job because I was on the verge of a total physical breakdown[/24]. My health had to come first. It’s been a struggle to adjust to a lower income, but [29]the improvement in my physical and emotional well-being has been worth every penny[/29]. I actually have time to cook healthy meals and exercise now. I’ve realized that no salary is worth sacrificing your health for. || Speaker 5: My main motivation for moving into a self-sufficient tiny house was environmental. [25]I wanted to drastically reduce my carbon footprint and live more sustainably[/25]. I grow my own vegetables and use solar power. The biggest surprise, though, has been [30]how much more I appreciate the few high-quality items I’ve kept[/30]. When you only have space for ten books, you choose them very carefully. It’s taught me to value quality over quantity in every aspect of my life, from the tools I use to the food I eat. It’s a much more intentional way of living.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) the main reason each speaker chose to downsize.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 felt the maintenance and cost of a large house were a burden.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 felt suffocated by clutter and the need to keep buying things.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted a lifestyle more in tune with the natural world.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 downsized because of a looming physical breakdown due to overwork.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to reduce her carbon footprint and live sustainably.

options.C: to relocate for a new job
options.D: to reduce the effort of home upkeep
options.E: to find a larger garden space
options.G: to escape a cycle of consumerism
options.H: to be nearer to family members

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker has found most beneficial about downsizing.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the financial freedom gained by eliminating a mortgage.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 highlights the mental clarity from living in a minimalist space.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 values the strong sense of camaraderie in the boating community.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 notes the improvement in her physical and emotional well-being.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 appreciates the quality of the few items she kept.

options.A: a greater appreciation for individual items
options.D: the chance to learn new practical skills
options.F: the ability to work from home
options.H: the support of a like-minded community
